title: Pulpo a la Gallega
subtitle: Galician octopus with smoked paprika and olive oil
excerpt: Tenderised octopus sliced onto a bed of boiled potato, dressed with smoked paprika and olive oil, and finished with sea salt. The defining dish of Galicia.
sourceNotes: Pulpo a la gallega (also called pulpo a feira — octopus at the fair) is Galicia's most celebrated dish, traditionally served on wooden boards at festivals. Documented in Claudia Roden, The Food of Spain (2011) and Penelope Casas, The Foods and Wines of Spain (1982).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The octopus can be cooked the day before and refrigerated. Slice cold and warm in the cooking water before dressing.

content.text: Octopus needs to be tenderised before it can be eaten with pleasure. The traditional Galician method is to beat the freshly caught octopus against rocks; the domestic equivalent is freezing — the ice crystals break down the fibrous muscle tissue. Most octopus sold in UK fishmongers has already been frozen. Ask; if it hasn't, freeze it overnight yourself before cooking.

The cooking is simple once you understand one thing: the octopus is dunked three times into boiling water before being submerged fully. This three-dunk method is traditional and is said to curl and set the tentacles. Whether or not it makes a real difference, it is part of the ritual.

items.prepNote: waxy, cooked separately in salted water

content.text: Bring a large pot of unsalted water to a rolling boil. Add the bay leaves and onion if using. Holding the octopus by the head, dip it into the boiling water for 5 seconds, lift it out, and repeat twice more. Then submerge fully. Cook at a gentle simmer (not a rolling boil, which toughens the flesh) for 45 to 60 minutes, until the thickest part of a tentacle yields easily when pierced with a skewer. Turn off the heat and leave the octopus in the water for 15 minutes.

Boil the potatoes separately in salted water until tender; drain and slice into 1 cm rounds. Arrange the potato slices on a wooden board or plate. Remove the octopus from the water. Use scissors to cut the tentacles into 1 cm pieces. Arrange over the potato. Drizzle generously with olive oil, dust with smoked paprika, and finish with sea salt flakes. Serve at room temperature.

items.cause: Not frozen before cooking; or not cooked long enough
items.fix: Freeze for at least 24 hours, defrost, then cook for the full time; some octopus takes up to 90 minutes

items.cause: Boiled at too high a temperature
items.fix: The water should be at a gentle simmer after the initial dunk — a hard boil causes the protein to contract and toughen

content.text: Pulpo a feira is named for the pulpeiras, the octopus women who have cooked the dish at Galician festivals and fairs for centuries. The dish travels from there to every restaurant in Spain but is best eaten in Galicia, on a wooden plate, with a glass of Albariño, in a stone-walled pulpería on a rainy afternoon.
